items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.correct: Someone who steps in to help
items.why: Correct answer: Someone who steps in to help. “A spectator” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: 0800 1111. “999” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 12. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
